title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Saline flips incentives for Oracle while Delta County pauses data centers for a year.

lines.text: Good evening and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're starting with a major reversal in Saline Township that could reshape what residents pay in taxes and see on their electric bills for years to come.
The township board flipped its earlier decision and restored Oracle's path to a big tax break on a multibillion-dollar campus.
What exactly changed in that vote, Graham? Why did they reverse course after setting limits before?
The board had previously capped incentives, but after lobbying from the company and supporters pointing to job creation, they walked it back today.
So now the project could move forward without paying full property taxes for years. What does that mean for the township's budget?
Residents could forgo millions in revenue that would have gone to schools, roads, and services. It's a classic trade-off between promised economic growth and lost local funding.
And the power and water angle — how big could that draw be for a hyperscale campus like this?
Even one facility like this pulls enormous amounts of electricity and water. In rural or growing areas that can strain the grid and local wells, potentially pushing up rates for everyone else.
We've seen this play out elsewhere in the state already. How does Saline's situation fit into the bigger pattern?
It's part of a wave where companies seek big breaks while towns weigh the long-term costs. Saline is just the latest example of that tension.
What happens next there? Is there more public input or legal steps before anything is finalized?
The reversal opens the door for the project to advance, but residents can still push back at future hearings or through local advocacy. Nothing is built yet.
And how does this compare to places that have hit the brakes entirely?
That's where Delta County comes in. They voted today for a full one-year ban on new data centers.
Why a full moratorium instead of just tighter rules?
Residents raised concerns about noise, water use, and pressure on the rural grid. Officials want time to draft permanent zoning before any large project can apply.
That makes sense for a sparsely populated area. One hyperscale site could affect rates and wells for years.
Exactly. The pause gives them breathing room to study the real impacts.
Meanwhile Wixom is holding its own pause in Oakland County. What drove that decision today?
The city council rejected an early end to the moratorium on a three-building complex, citing health risks, noise, and resource strain that could raise costs for residents.
So they're prioritizing protection over speed. How common is this kind of local pushback becoming?
It's spreading. Yesterday Lovells Township in Crawford County also enacted a year-long ban for the same reasons.
And the new MLive map that launched today — how does that help people track all this?
It shows every active fight by township and county, including megawatts demanded and potential tax deals. You can see how your area compares to others in real time.
That's useful for folks trying to understand what's coming their way.
Now shifting to yesterday's developments. Williamston Township shelved its data center zoning rules for now.
What does shelving actually mean here? No timeline at all?
They postponed action, leaving uncertainty high for nearby homes and farms over noise, power lines, and water. Officials gave no return date.
Hundreds showed up at a hearing there to grill officials on traffic, noise, and electric rates. Strong opposition came through.
No vote was taken, so the board has more time to decide next steps with that public pressure in mind.
Nearby Williamstown Township also delayed its rules until August after a hearing on setbacks, noise, water, and wildlife.
Planning commissioners tabled the ordinance. Residents voiced worries about impacts on local resources.
Pittsfield Township is moving the other direction — drafting new rules ahead of a potential hyperscale project.
They're setting zoning and performance standards now so guardrails are in place before any formal application. The site could pull hundreds of megawatts.
That proactive approach might avoid some of the fights we're seeing elsewhere.
On the legal side, Monroe Township is facing a $300 million lawsuit from a developer after banning data centers.
What could that mean for taxpayers if the township loses?
Higher taxes or legal costs are possible. It shows how these zoning battles are attracting big-money litigation in southeast Michigan.
Quite the contrast to places choosing moratoriums instead.
Now let's look ahead to meetings residents can actually attend tonight.
Oxford Township Planning Commission is discussing a draft data center ordinance as a starting point for public input.
No action is expected, just the beginning of feedback. The Charter Township of Oxford has the same item on its agenda.
Good chance for people to weigh in early before anything gets locked in.
That's the picture across Michigan right now — towns balancing jobs, revenue, power demands, and quality of life one decision at a time.
Thanks for walking through it all, Graham.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Saline Township Reverses on Oracle Tax Breaks as Power and Water Costs Loom for Residents
